Calculation of Potential Vorticity on the ICON Grid
Tobias Selz
Ludwig-Maximilians-Universit¨ at M¨ unchen
Abstract
This article describes the calculation of Potential Vorticity (PV) on the ICON grid. The
algorithm has been implemented into the model and the PV is available as output variable on
model-, pressure-, height-, and isentropic surfaces.
Keywords: Potential Vorticity
Correspondence to: Tobias Selz (tobias.selz@lmu.de)
1. Introduction
Potential vorticity (PV) is deﬁned according to Ertel (1942) by
PV = 1
ρ (∇× va)·∇θ,
with the air density ρ, the three-dimensional absolute wind va and the potential temperature θ.
The absolute wind is deﬁnded as the sum of the wind relative to the rotating earth, v = (u,v,w )
and the velocity of the rotation itself,vr = Ω×r. With the Coriolis parameterf =∇×(Ω×r) = 2Ω
the PV can be written as
PV = 1
ρ (∇× v + f)·∇θ. (1)
To derive a numerical method to calculate PV on the ICON grid, equation (1) will be transformed
to spherical coordinates, locally rotated to the edge-coordinate system of the grid and ﬁnally
transformed to a generalized vertical coordinate system. The resulting equation can then be used
for discretisation with already implemented standard ICON methods.
Reports on ICON - Issue 002 3
Potential Vorticity Tobias Selz
2. Spherical coordinates
Following Vallis (2006), the curl of a vector ﬁeld B in geographic spherical coordinates can be
written as
∇× B =
(
∂yBz−∂zBy− By

where the latitude ϑ, the longitude φ and the distance to the earth midpoint r have already been
replaced with the usual local-cartesian coordinates x =rφ cosϑ, y =rϑ and z =r−re, with the
earth radius re. With this, the PV on the sphere is given by
ρ PV =



3. Local rotation to edge coordinate system
At the edge midpoints on the ICON grid, an orthogonal coordinate system is deﬁned by the vector
n, pointing normal to the edge, the vector t, pointing tangential to the edge and the vertical
direction k, with t× n = k and k pointing away from the earth midpoint. The horizontal part of
the scalar product in (2) is invariant under a rotation of the coordinate system around the z-axis.
In addition, the vorticity can equally be expressed in such a rotated coordinate system. Thus
with y→n and x→t the PV equation can be rewritten as
ρ PV =


∂nw−∂zvn− vn
re+z +ft
∂zvt−∂tw + vt
re+z +fn
∂tvn−∂nvt + u tanϑ
re+z +fz

·


∂tθ
∂nθ
∂zθ

.
4. Generalized vertical coordinate
The ﬁnal step is to transform PV to the models vertical coordinate, h : (t,n,η )→ (t,n,z ), with
z =z(t,n,η ). The Jacobian D of this transformation reads
Dh = ∂(t,n,z )
∂(t,n,η ) =


1 0 0
0 1 0
∂z

With the shortcut γ =∂z/∂η this gives us the equation for PV in the model coordinate system:
ρ PV =


Note that the wind vector (vt,vn,w ) in the model is still expressed in the original ( t,n,z )-system,
which implies that the wind components have to be treated as scalar ﬁelds.
5. Implementation
The calculation of the PV is done in the subroutine compute field pv which is deﬁned in the ﬁle
mo util phys.f90. Regardless of the setting for the dynamical core the PV calculation makes

catalog_entry.summary: This paper describes the calculation of Potential Vorticity (PV) on the ICON grid, detailing the algorithm's implementation and its availability as an output variable in various model configurations.

variables.long_name: Potential vorticity calculated on the ICON grid
